scenario_name: Scenario 21 — Multi-Parcel Tracking Partial Delivery Detection
justification: Meets all pass criteria: Shipment vs Parcel clearly distinguished as aggregate root vs child entity with separate lifecycles. BR-2 and BR-3 explicitly state shipment status is derived/projected from parcel states, never independently set — the critical domain insight. Parcel state machine (BR-4) defines 8+ states including Created, Picked Up, In Transit, Out for Delivery, Delivered, Lost, Damaged, Held, Returned. Business invariants cover partial delivery detection (BR-3, AC-1), lost parcel escalation (BR-9, AC-3), and parcel-level dispute/refund granularity (BR-8, AC-5). Eight non-trivial edge cases surfaced including out-of-order tracking events, customs holds, mid-transit repackaging, and cross-shipment parcel swaps. Ten detailed acceptance criteria in Given/When/Then format provided.

observations.text: BR-2 is unambiguous: 'Shipment status MUST be derived from the aggregate state of its Parcels — never set independently' with justification explaining why independent status creates corrupt state
BR-3 provides explicit derivation mapping from parcel state combinations to shipment status, including the non-obvious 'Closed with Exceptions' terminal state
Correctly distinguishes invariants from business policies (e.g., BR-7 notification is not invariant, BR-5 terminal immutability is)
Edge cases go well beyond happy path — parcel splitting mid-transit, cross-shipment swaps, out-of-order carrier events, and cancellation after partial delivery
Acceptance criteria are concrete and testable, covering partial delivery, full delivery, lost parcel, terminal state immutability, POD requirements, and dispute creation
Business impact analysis identifies migration risk for existing single-tracking-number shipments and API backward compatibility — practical operational concerns
Ubiquitous language section precisely defines 10 domain terms with clear distinctions (e.g., Shipment Reference is NOT a tracking number)
Does not explicitly model DeliveryAttempt as a value object in the entity identification — it appears in AC-7 but is not formally defined in the ubiquitous language or entity model
Missing explicit discussion of idempotency for duplicate tracking scans, though out-of-order events are covered
